title: Stick Weld Defects Causes and Solutions Guide
summary: These pages cover common stick welding defects including penetration issues, porosity, spatter, burn-through, slag, crooked beads, and weld adhesion problems. Each defect is presented with a profile view and a list of possible causes with corrective actions. This section helps operators diagnose and resolve weld quality problems.
key_facts: Excess penetration or burn-through is caused by workpiece overheating or welding speed too slow.
Inadequate penetration can result from incorrect technique, insufficient heat, or thick workpieces.
Slag shields the weld from impurities and must be cleaned off with a chipping hammer and wire brush.
Porosity is caused by a dirty workpiece, unclean fill material, or inconsistent welding speed.
A crooked or wavy bead results from inaccurate welding or inconsistent welding speed.
Fine spatter is normal; grainy and large spatter indicates a problem.
Excessive spatter is caused by a dirty workpiece or unclean fill material.
Burn-through occurs when base material melts away, leaving a hole in the weld.
Weld not adhering properly shows gaps between weld and previous bead or workpiece.
Bend at joint is caused by improper clamping or excessive heat during welding.
Thick workpieces should be beveled, allowed a slight gap, and welded in several passes.
Tack welds can be made to hold workpieces and prevent bending at the joint.
warnings: Slag is a necessary part of a stick weld. It shields the weld from impurities. Clean off the slag with the Chipping Hammer and Wire Brush after welding.
Make certain that fill material and Electrode are clean and free from oil, coatings, and other residues.
